serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Aloo Jeera is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate side-dish portion served alongside dal and flatbread at an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN), ICMR, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Aloo Jeera are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods using component-level data for boiled potato, cumin seeds, and cooking oil/ghee in proportions consistent with standard North Indian recipe formulations. Values are consistent with the input enriched_json record and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for potato and cumin seed components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Jeera as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white potato (GI ~72, glucose reference), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Fat and fibre from cumin and oil moderately attenuate glycemic response; however, the base GI of 72 (high) is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 13.33 × 1.5) / 100 = 14.4 per 150 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 216.67 mg per 100 g; potassium 320 mg per 100 g provides a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit added salt during preparation and keep portion size to 150 g or less; the naturally high potassium content from potatoes supports blood pressure regulation, but sodium from seasoning must be controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and significant starch content (13.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g) drive postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict Aloo Jeera to small portions (≤100 g), pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legumes to blunt glycemic response, and prefer preparations using cooled boiled potatoes which have higher resistant starch content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (6 g per 100 g) with low saturated fat (0.83 g); cholesterol is negligible; cumin and turmeric provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Aloo Jeera in moderate portions prepared with minimal oil or mustard oil; the low saturated fat and absence of cholesterol are favourable, but overall caloric density and sodium should be monitored.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cumin and turmeric possess anti-inflammatory properties; potatoes are nightshades which may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Most individuals with rheumatoid arthritis can consume Aloo Jeera in moderation; those who find nightshade vegetables exacerbate joint symptoms should monitor their response and reduce or eliminate potato-based dishes accordingly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Jeera is a classic North Indian vegetarian side dish consisting of parboiled potatoes that are cubed and sautéed in oil or ghee with a generous quantity of cumin seeds (jeera). The preparation begins by tempering whole cumin seeds in hot oil until they splutter and release their characteristic earthy, warm aroma. Cubed boiled potatoes are then added and tossed to coat them in the fragrant oil. The dish is seasoned with turmeric for colour, green chilies for mild heat, and amchoor (dried mango powder) for a subtle tartness. The result is a dry, lightly spiced potato dish with a golden exterior, soft interior, and a dominant cumin fragrance. Nutritionally, Aloo Jeera provides approximately 117 kcal per 100 g, with 2.67 g protein, 6 g fat, and 13.33 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of potassium (320 mg per 100 g) and vitamin C (13.33 mg per 100 g). The dish has a high glycemic index (~72) due to its potato base, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally vegetarian, gluten-free, and low in saturated fat. Aloo Jeera is commonly served as a side dish with dal and phulka or paratha at breakfast, lunch, or dinner across Northern India, and is also a popular travel food paired with pooris.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component-level data for Indian foods and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for potato and cumin seed components. No single approved-source entry exists for Aloo Jeera as a composite dish; values represent a defensible recipe-level estimate. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white potato as the closest analogous food.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Aloo Jeera as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this vegetable-oil or ghee-based preparation per ICMR-NIN and USDA data. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible for a plant-based preparation; if ghee is used, trace cholesterol may be present but is below reportable threshold in standard serving sizes. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard vegetarian formulation. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as potatoes and cumin seeds contain negligible vitamin D and no fortified ingredients are used. 7. GI source is an analogous estimate based on boiled white potato (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Aloo Jeera as a composite dish was found in approved sources. 8. Input portions contained duplicate entries; deduplicated to two distinct portions: 150 g (standard serving) and 250 g (restaurant plate). 9. Input sources included 'Diabetes Foundation (India)' for GI, which is not on the approved source list; GI has been re-anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled potato as the analogous food.
